data.title: Bitcoin Briefly Dips Below $80,000
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Risk-off moves

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) dropped below $80,000 overnight, losing more than 25% since its record high on January 20 - the day of President Trump's inauguration - in a dramatic price correction, as looming tariffs drive safe-haven demand. Other cryptocurrencies have also been weighed by the prevailing global risk-off sentiment, and crypto-related stocks are expected to see some pressure.

Fading hype: Bitcoin had been one of the most popular Trump trades, given expectations of a friendlier regulatory environment under the new administration. But investors are now moving towards safer assets as Trump ramps up his tariff threats. "Instead of serving as an alternative to fiat instability, as its proponents have long claimed, crypto has once again been treated like other high-risk assets and sold off accordingly," SA analyst TMC Research noted in a recent analysis.

If the risk-off sentiment continues, bitcoin could lose all its gains from the post-election rally and outflows from crypto ETFs could swell further. Tuesday alone saw more than $1B pulled out of spot bitcoin ETFs, the largest single-day cash exodus for the ETFs since their debut in last January. The crypto fear and greed index, which tracks trader sentiment in the sector, is now flashing "Extreme Fear."

Hope remains: Still, crypto bulls are hopeful that bitcoin will rebound soon and a bottom for its price is nearing. Geoffrey Kendrick, head of digital assets research at Standard Chartered, told CNBC on Thursday that he still expects the top crypto to reach $200,000 this year as the industry sees more institutionalization and regulatory clarity.

What else is happening...

Trump greenlights Mexico, Canada and China tariffs for March.

Trump: U.K. could dodge U.S. tariffs if trade deal is reached.

Quantum leap: Amazon (AMZN) introduces new 'Ocelot' chip.

Nasdaq 100 falls from peak, Alphabet (GOOG) now oversold.

CFPB drops lawsuits against Capital One (COF) and others.

Verizon's DEI practices, Frontier (FYBR) deal under FCC lens.

NEC's Hassett: DOGE Dividend makes a great deal of sense.

Elliott ramps up pressure on BP as strategic reset disappoints.

Sycamore looking to split Walgreens (WBA) into three parts.

Examining the growing online push to boycott Tesla (TSLA).

Today's Markets

In Asia, Japan -2.9%. Hong Kong -3.3%. China -2%. India -1.9%.
In Europe, at midday, London +0.3%. Paris -0.3%. Frankfurt -0.2%.
Futures at 6:30, Dow +0.3%. S&P +0.4%. Nasdaq +0.3%. Crude -1.5% to $69.29. Gold -0.8% to $2,874.10. Bitcoin -6.7% to $80,620.
Ten-year Treasury Yield unchanged at 4.26%.

Today's Economic Calendar

08:30 AM International Trade in Goods (Advance)
08:30 AM Personal Income and Outlays
08:30 AM Retail Inventories (Advance)
08:30 AM Wholesale Inventories (Advance)
09:45 AM Chicago PMI
01:00 PM Baker Hughes Rig Count
03:00 PM Farm Prices
10:15 PM Fed's Goolsbee Speech

Companies reporting earnings today »

data.title: Great Lakes Dredge & Dock: Recent Correction Not Yet A Buying Opportunity
data.description:
As the largest North American dredging company out there, Great Lakes Dock & Dredge (GLDD) has a fairly unique business proposition. Founded way back in 1890, the company has stood the test of time, and its operations based out of Houston have given it plenty of exposure to Gulf-based economic activities such as the offshore energy sector.

However, GLDD stock has largely gotten stuck on the choppy waters, with shares delivering little meaningful appreciation for decades now:

The stock reached a peak in 2021 tied to increased federal government infrastructure spending, but capsized shortly thereafter. A more recent rally in late 2024 ended abruptly following mixed earnings and worries around future government spending.

All this leads to the question of whether there is potentially either a bounce-back trade here following the recent 30% correction, and also whether the company will ever be able to deliver better long-term shareholder value creation.

What Caused The Recent Sell-Off

Zooming in on the above chart to the past year, we see that GLDD stock was on a strong run in 2024, but momentum abruptly flipped over the past two months:

The stock is now down more than 25% year-to-date.

GLDD shares reached their 52-week highs in December, when optimism was running high around small-cap stocks following the presidential election. Since then, the iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM) has pulled back more than 10% off its recent highs, and this likely has contributed to negative trading in GLDD stock. In particular, the new administration's federal spending cuts are weighing on sentiment around domestic industrial companies. Great Lakes Dredge & Dock's reliance on almost exclusively U.S.-based revenues with a significant government-derived revenue component causes it to be exposed to these concerns.

In February, the company released its Q4 earnings. These were reasonably strong, but clearly not as favorable as the market had anticipated.

EPS of 29 cents beat expectations by six cents, though this slipped a hair from the 32 cents it reported for the same quarter of 2023. Revenues of $203 million were up a solid 11.6% year-over-year but fell well short of the $211 million that analysts had been expecting.

One point of concern is that the firm's backlog has almost stopped growing. It has been up sharply year-over-year in recent quarters. This gave investors confidence that future revenues would continue trending higher, as the company historically converts backlog into revenue fairly quickly. However, sequentially from Q3 to Q4, backlog inched up merely from $1.21 billion to $1.24 billion. This suggests that the current cycle may be peaking in terms of dredging demand.

There are still longer-term catalysts which could help the company operationally. For example, the Trump Administration seems likely to take a more favorable stance toward new LNG export projects. Great Lakes Docks & Dredge has a significant business opportunity tied to LNG terminals. On the other hand, the company also historically has done business with offshore wind projects which could be harmed by a more restrictive stance toward green energy financing going forward.

Longer-Term: Limited Revenue Growth, Uneven Profitability, Weak Cash Flows

It's quite possible that GLDD stock will be able to recover from its recent correction. Short-term sentiment is fairly depressed, and GLDD stock has made numerous sharp moves in the past. It's not hard to see a scenario where, for example, increased visibility around government infrastructure spending could cause a rebound in the price of Great Lakes Dredge & Dock stock.

As a long-term investment, however, I have concerns around the company's appeal. For one, the company's revenue picture, while lumpy, has not been trending in a great direction:

Business seemingly peaked about a decade ago and has struggled to recapture that prior high. Keep in mind the above chart isn't in real terms, either, meaning that the company's real revenue generation capability has been slipping even more given the mounting level of inflation since 2020.

Not surprisingly, this sort of uneven revenue picture has helped lead to wild swings in profitability. On a GAAP earnings basis, Great Lakes Dredge & Dock has lost money five out of the past ten years. Generally, investors are not willing to assign high multiples to companies with such variable levels of profitability. Even during years with strong net income, investors tend to look around the corner and wonder how much of a downturn there will be during the next cyclical swing.

Making matters worse, this is a fairly capital-intensive business, with Great Lakes Dredge & Dock having to invest substantial sums to build new equipment and keep its assets modernized. As a recent investor slide points out, the company has added three major vessels since 2018:

Unfortunately, this capital expenditure has seemingly not moved the needle much in terms of improving the company's underlying revenues or profitability.

As a result of its uneven profitability combined with CAPEX spending, the company has produced cumulative negative net free cash flow over the past decade:

Aside from a brief run in 2019, Great Lakes Dredge & Dock rarely generates much of anything in the way of substantial free cash flow. Even in 2021 when the stock price was at its highest, underlying cash flow generation was quite weak.

As a result of these weak cash flows, Great Lakes Dredge & Dock has not been able to return much of anything to shareholders. It has not paid a dividend since 2012. And the company doesn't repurchase shares. Rather, the share count slowly climbs over time, rising from 60 million in 2015 to about 67 million today.

Ultimately, for a company to deliver substantial value generation for shareholders, it needs to be able to generate significant cash flows which can then be turned into dividends or share repurchases. The underlying economics of Great Lakes Dredge & Dock's business model simply haven't been able to do that over the past decade.

GLDD Stock Verdict

I am sympathetic to the view that GLDD stock has been overly sold off on its recent results along with fears around government spending cuts.

On that latter point, I'd note that the company's president and CEO, Lasse Petterson, addressed this concern directly in the most recent conference call, saying:

"Yes, the, the situation with DOGE, I don't know who knows where that goes. But as we understand they are starting to look at the defense spending this week. We have this strong backlog which is a water project, it is funded projects. So the dredging backlog will be executed during 2025. That is, we're very certain on that and also into 2026. So if there is some efforts to delay the bid market for that, is now coming out, typically in Q2 and Q3 of 2025, the impact on us will be minimal, if any for 2025. It may impact backlog generation for 2026."

Reading from that, it seems 2025 spending is pretty well locked in already. While there may be a negative headwind for 2026, that remains to be seen, and a lot can change around government policy between now and then.

Given this fact, I am at a hold rating on GLDD stock today on the premise that the recent drop from $12.50 to $8.20 looks like a knee-jerk overreaction. I wouldn't be surprised if the stock traded back up to $9-$10 over the next quarter as folks give the company credit for its relatively strong backlog and prospects over the next year.

That said, in the bigger picture, I struggle to get comfortable with a company that simply hasn't been able to demonstrate an ability to generate consistent free cash flow for such a long period of time. It's possible that some structural changes will happen in the market, such as increased demand for LNG services, which bolsters overall sector profitability. But other catalysts, like a past cycle of offshore wind project spending, have seemingly come and gone without materially altering Great Lakes Dredge & Dock's long-term profitability metrics.

I think you could make an argument that in a world where the price of oil heads back above $100/barrel and natural gas trades sharply higher that you'd see a renaissance in offshore energy sector activity. This would likely give a meaningful tailwind to the company's results. The stock, for what it's worth, was trading much better between 2010 and 2014, and then slumped sharply once the price of crude tanked in 2015. However, if you are highly bullish on oil and gas prices, there are probably more direct ways to capture that upside in other equities as opposed to GLDD stock.

data.title: 3 Investments I'd Hold For Life
data.description:
If I was limited to making a very few investment decisions in my life, what would those be?

Recently, I have been thinking about this question a lot. Nobody likes being limited, definitely, not us investors, who often have the urge to act on the latest data or earnings report, but evidence points to the fact that, actually, the portfolios of "dead people" are performing the best.

The reason?

Very simple. The portfolios of "dead people" benefit from inactivity and lack of cognitive bias, avoiding emotional decision-making based on market fluctuations or buying at highs and selling at lows, with virtually an infinite time horizon.

This brings me to the point of the "Buy & Hold" strategy, famed by Warren Buffett, as this approach heavily benefits from compounding and eliminates unnecessary costs such as taxes (in taxable accounts) and brokerage fees.

Just imagine the economic and financial news all around us about inflation, interest rates, growth vs. value, trade wars, tariffs, supply chain disruptions, consumer sentiment etc. All this news forces us to make day-to-day adjustments to our portfolios, which often prove to be inferior to our returns.

History proves to us that simple, long-term investing is one of the best tools to build wealth, but it's not as easy as it may sound. In order to unlock attractive returns, one has to choose the right investment vehicles, either ETFs, individual stocks, real estate, bonds, or commodities. In fact, being diversified across different asset classes is the best way to avoid market turbulence and reduce volatility, but often, diversification is inferior, particularly to smaller portfolios with less capital.

For instance, my investment style is all about total returns. I am 30 years old, and I am actively adding capital to my investment portfolio while buying local real estate. I don't rely on any distributions of my portfolio. Instead, I reinvest all the cash I earn via my investments to build a snowball. My strategy is all about patience and compounding, yet I value peace of mind and sleep-well-at-night.

That's why I decided to write today's article on three investments I would hold for life while creating some asset balance and geographical diversification.

This list shouldn't be treated as a "buy list," as many of the equities I mention are simply not currently trading at attractive valuations with a favorable risk/reward profile, but I want to show you what assets are worth looking into.

1st Investment

One of the core investments, that I recommend to any age group of investors, is to allocate 20-50% of one's portfolio into broader market ETFs, such as:

Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO)

Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ)

Vanguard Total Stock Market Index Fund ETF (VTI)

Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD)

Even as the broader market indices are currently top tech-heavy, over the years, the allocation to specific companies changes, and the various sectors and geographical exposure provide a good layer of diversification, reducing reliance on one specific company/sector and over the long term, diversified ETF generates superior risk-adjusted ROR.

Below, you can see the change in the top 10 holdings of the S&P 500 over 40 years:

The benefit of having an ETF as a core holding in one's portfolio comes from a low-cost basis with an expense ratio ranging from 0.03% to 0.20%, which enhances the overall returns in the long term, as compounding works its magic. Higher fees, generally associated with frequent trading over long periods of time, e.g., 20-30 years, can result in thousands of dollars lost.

However, what I particularly find attractive is the simplified portfolio management. Instead of owning Mag7 as individual stocks (yes, I do own individually the ones I am overweight), ETF makes it easy to dollar-cost-average or exit the position in one click.

The dollar-cost-averaging strategy is, particularly, useful for investors like me, who invest a fixed sum on a monthly basis from an earned income.

The last decade returns were simply stellar, but I am skeptical we will see similar results over the next 10 years as we are starting today from high valuations and a lot of uncertainty around the world:

Among the ETFs, I also included the golden standard of dividend income, SCHD, which I am a fan of due to its defensive nature, with more mature companies, oriented more towards value. Especially in times like these, when the top 10 holdings make up 35% of S&P 500 and 50% of QQQ, having a wider exposure towards legacy industries might prove prudent.

For instance, in QQQ you will find Costco (COST) trading at 60x its P/E with EPS around 10% annually and Palantir (PLTR) valued at 207x its P/E with a EPS growth of around 20-30% annually, which is far too expensive for my liking, but this is the downside of ETF's that one cannot eliminate the non-desired stock (due to their valuation, not business).

Around 30% of my portfolio is in VOO and I recently tactically exited my small QQQ position around 1-2% of my total portfolio.

Personally, I like a 30% allocation to VOO and a 20% allocation to SCHD for its 3.53% dividend yield. In today's pricey market landscape, I think positioning more defensively is a valid strategy. If we would see a 10-20% correction in the broader market indices, I would rotate the allocation towards 40-50% in VOO.

2nd Investment

Perhaps surprisingly to many, one of my core investments in my portfolio is the European luxury companies.

It's true that Europe has much less to offer from a stock market perspective compared to the US, particularly in the technology space outside of ASML (ASML). As a European myself, I am not proud of this fact, and owning European businesses with heavy sales exposure in Europe, in my opinion, is a great way to earn submarket returns, but owning European businesses with 70-80% of sales exposed to other continents is what I look for.

European luxury businesses are one of the oldest businesses worldwide, not only surviving but thriving today as the world's middle-class and ultra-wealthy class grows.

One of the reasons why luxury brands are attractive to my taste is their defensive nature, at least for some.

The industry is currently navigating a slowdown after COVID-19 boost in sales fueled by stimulus checks and extra savings, but we already see the winners, such as Hermes (OTCPK:HESAY), catering to the ultra-wealthy being resilient to economic swings and cyclicality, reporting as much as 19% growth in Q4.

On the other side of the coin, we have LVMH (OTCPK:LVMHF), which I see as a small Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) but of fashion. The company owns 70+ heritage brands, growing organically and via M&A, but with such a wide portfolio, the business mostly caters predominantly to aspirational buyers, who curtailed their luxury spending on goods.

Perhaps you might be thinking, that luxury is not the right industry to be in, in order to earn market-beating returns. But let me prove you wrong:

Over the past 15 years, Hermes shares appreciated 20.5% annually, whereas the tech-heavy S&P 500 has appreciated "only" 12.5% annually. Hermes' stock performance is on par with Apple, which was one of the success stories of 2010s in US.

Then we have LVMH, which over the same duration is underperforming the US stock market at 11.8%, as the company's top and bottom line suffered as a result of the slowdown in the industry.

Yes, this shows that LVMH is much more cyclical compared to Hermes, which experienced only 1 year of negative growth in the past 20 years, but this also presents an opportunity to scoop up shares of LVMH at a better valuation with recovery likely in H2 2025 and 2026.

This brings me to the way the businesses operate.

Luxury goods are somewhat of a different animal in terms of sales because the truly luxury brands must create a desire to buy the pricey items, achieved via scarcity, limiting the supply of the most demanded products while creating artificial demand for their other product lines by making customers spend thousands to establish their purchasing history with the boutiques to get invited to purchase the desired goods. A great example is the Birkin Bag by Hermes.

In order to maintain the scarcity status and control the inventory, both Hermes and LVMH fully control their supply chain from material sourcing through artisan production to sales via company-owned boutiques, destroying any non-sold inventory.

However, this also brings me to risks. Since the brands need to retain their exclusive image, the businesses cannot flood the market with their goods, meaning they can only sell a limited number of goods, which, for instance, for Hermes, is further limited by the production capacity. Yet, the demand far outstrips the supply.

If one cannot sell more volume, the other strategy is to raise prices, but particularly for LVMH, which doesn't cater to the ultra-wealthy, this is a risk because we already see prices relatively high, which certainly impacts its demand, yet one cannot go back, hitting the discount racks.

This also brings me to the point of Berkshire Hathaway, and recently reading one of BRK's annual letters, Buffet mentioned the benefit of having long-term-minded investors with few shares trading hands in the market. With Hermes' ownership structure of 67% float owned by the family dynasty and LVMH's 48% owned by the Arnault family, the shares are much less volatile, and both focus on shareholder-friendly policies.

As you can see, the businesses are very enticing to own in one's portfolio, and that's why I own LVMH in mine, yet I missed the boat for Hermes,' and the shares priced at 62x P/E are far too expensive for my liking. Nonetheless, this is one of the top companies on my watchlist to pull the trigger when either the EPS catch-up or correction comes.

3rd Investment

The last investment category that I own in my portfolio, and likely will for life are non-cyclical businesses with, industry-leading profitability.

Yes, these businesses rarely trade at cheap valuations, as the market recognized the super quality of these blue chips, but I am of the opinion that paying a fair price for a great business is better than paying a low price for a fair business.

Most of these businesses are also dividend growth stocks, which makes it attractive to hold for life because, eventually, one will need to live either from the capital after an asset sale or from a passive income; I prefer the latter.

When investing for the long term in individual companies, as I do, the key is to select companies with healthy balance sheets, with little leverage, and preferably lots of cash to invest in new opportunities at an ROI higher than the market's 10-12% ROR.

The two perfect examples from my portfolio that I have held since I started investing and will for life as long their fundamentals or outlook doesn't change are Visa (V) and Mastercard (MA).

The companies operate as a duopoly in the growing payment space as a cashless society becomes the norm in Western countries. Likewise, as the money supply increases, both work as a hedge against inflation due to their %-based fee structure from transactions.

You might be thinking that their earnings would be cyclical due to economic cycles, but in fact, Visa's earnings dropped only once in 15 years.

...and similarly for Mastercard.

Yes, if a recession of a similar magnitude as the Great Financial Crisis hits, neither Visa nor Mastercard will be immune, but I would see this as a perfect opportunity to add more to my existing positions.

Both companies enjoy software-like profit margins, with Mastercard trailing Visa, due to their attempts to gain market share via promotions.

Another great example of a resilient, non-cyclical compounder is MSCI (MSCI).

With a market cap of $45B, it's a relatively small player in the financial industry, but a major beneficiary of the shift from actively managed investment funds to passive investments such as ETFs.

As long as this trend continues and the market prospers over the long-term MSCI is set to keep growing.

MSCI's crown jewel business is the index segment, where the business earns asset-based fees with over $15T in benchmarked assets. The business enjoys a 95% retention rate, and over 75% of the revenue is generated via subscriptions, making it, once again, non-cyclical.

The issue here is the valuation, at 37.5x its P/E I wouldn't recommend buying the shares today, but certainly keep it high up on the watchlist.

A few more examples falling into this category of wide-moat compounders in my portfolio are Microsoft (MSFT) and MercadoLibre (MELI).

The good thing is that with the exception of MELI, all pay small dividends but quickly growing:

  • Visa: Div. Yield of 0.67%, 5Y growth rate of 15.08%
  • Mastercard: 0.54%, 14.54%
  • MSCI: 1.24%, 20.30%
  • Microsoft: 0.83%, 10.25%

Eventually, these small yields may convert to more attractive distributions, but for the time being, I prefer the companies reinvest the cash back into business to unlock the compounding.

Investor's Takeaway

In this article, I have shown you a few investments that I'll hold for life, such as broader market ETFs, luxury goods businesses based in Europe and non-cyclical, wide-moat businesses with industry-leading profitability.

I have not included in this piece any REITs, BDCs or MLPs, even though I see great value in many of these, but currently, most of my passive income is flowing from a physical real estate I purchased in the area where I live.

Certainly, one can make a case that US treasuries or commodities should be part of a long-term-oriented portfolio as well, but I use those rather for tactical allocation as I see fit.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Datacenters Balancing Sustainability Goals With Accelerating AI Demand
data.description:
Major datacenter build-outs aimed at meeting the rising demand for AI are complicating efforts by hyperscalers to lower their carbon footprints. But as much as AI is creating this problem, it also may be at least part of the solution.

US datacenters are projected to consume nearly 800 TWh in 2030 — more than double 2024 levels, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence 451 Research's Datacenter Services & Infrastructure Market Monitor & Forecast. Roughly 30% of that projected power consumption is expected to come directly from hyperscale datacenters like those operated by Alphabet Inc.'s Google LLC, Meta Platforms Inc. Microsoft Corp. (MSFT) and Amazon.com Inc. (AMZN).

A large part of this increased power consumption is due to generative AI. Generative queries and workloads consume 10 to 30 times more energy than traditional or task-specific AI tasks. With US companies looking to stay ahead in the AI race — especially in the face of competing generative AI platforms such as China's Hangzhou DeepSeek Artificial Intelligence Co. Ltd. (DEEPSEEK) - hyperscalers are in the difficult position of trying to balance their rising energy needs with the aggressive net-zero targets they set before generative AI took off. Their existing strategies, such as renewable energy projects and carbon removal credits, are expanding and evolving as companies rethink their approach to carbon-free energy. Alternative energy sources and AI-driven workload management are also being deployed.

"To solve this, we need breakthroughs," said Sam Uyeno, a partner at technology consulting firm West Monroe who specializes in energy and utilities. "There is not a clear pathway right now to having everybody achieve their net-zero goals, including the high-tech sector and hyperscalers."

Supply and demand

One factor that is both a challenge and opportunity is that the current energy supply is not enough to meet expected demand.

Nearly 60% of global tech companies, datacenter developers and energy providers do not believe that the current pace of energy deployment in the US is enough to meet the energy demand caused by AI, according to a KPMG survey. In the same survey, 55% of hyperscalers and datacenter developers said they were willing to pay up to 50% more of their current expenditures on electricity costs to expand capacity.

With companies having to look for new energy sources and willing to pay more for them, that could accelerate the tech industry's move toward renewables and clean tech, said Angie Gildea, national sector lead for energy at KPMG.

This is already visible at hyperscalers such as Microsoft.

"The rapid growth in demand for AI innovation to fuel the next frontiers of discovery has provided us with an opportunity to redesign our infrastructure systems, from datacenters to servers to silicon, with efficiency and sustainability at the forefront," Mark Russinovich, chief technology officer and technical fellow for Microsoft Azure, Microsoft's global enterprise-grade cloud platform, wrote in a September 2024 blog post.

Still, it is notable that Microsoft and other hyperscalers have reduced the frequency with which they mention sustainability terms on their earnings conference calls in recent years following a peak in late 2021, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data. Meanwhile, since the launch of OpenAI LLC's ChatGPT in late 2022, the number of mentions of generative AI-related terms has skyrocketed.

And while 94% of KPMG respondents are implementing some form of carbon mitigation efforts for their datacenters, 60% said they are at least somewhat likely to delay their sustainability targets due to the increasing energy demands placed on datacenters.

"Businesses are really between a rock and a hard place for where they put their resources and their capital," Gildea said. "Not only do they have this growing need for electricity by things like AI and datacenters, but we also have the growing need to make our infrastructure more resilient with some of these extreme weather events that we're seeing."

Crossing the Rubicon

Despite the shifting public focus toward generative AI, sustainability is still a priority for hyperscalers, experts said.

"I truly think we've crossed the Rubicon in this regard," said Hans Dyke, an attorney and partner who advises clients on energy infrastructure at Bracewell. "In terms of the energy grid and the energy technology markets and what our clients do, building infrastructure and owning infrastructure assets, I don't see a real risk in the sustainability piece going away. It's just too embedded now into the way we finance projects, in the way corporates procure clean energy."

Google (GOOGL) still says it aims to achieve net-zero emissions across all of its operations and value chain by 2030, despite having reported a 13% year-over-year increase in total greenhouse gas emissions for 2023, the most recent year reported. Meta similarly aims to reach net-zero emissions across its value chain by 2030, and Microsoft has committed to being carbon negative by the same year, meaning it will remove more carbon dioxide from the atmosphere than it emits. Amazon is targeting 2040 for net-zero carbon emissions across its operations.

The way these companies are approaching their goals, however, has shifted to include a broader definition of carbon-free energy sources.

In particular, nuclear energy will likely play a key role in powering the country's electric grid to meet future datacenter demand, industry experts said. The US Energy Department is looking to lower barriers to entry and streamline permitting for companies like Austin, Texas-based Aalo Atomics, which is working with the DOE to build an experimental 10-MW microreactor at the Idaho National Laboratory expected to go online by 2027.

"One of the things that excites me the most about this moment with nuclear power, in particular, is that it too, has a technology leap forward that can check all of the public trust boxes and provide lower economics and help with climate change," said Jon Guidroz, senior vice president of commercialization and strategy at Aalo Atomics. "So if we're successful, we untether humanity's growth from our impact on Earth and enable a thriving ecosystem broadly. And I think that's a once-in-a-generation opportunity. That's going to create a tremendous amount of shared value across all phases of humanity."

In 2024 alone, Google, Amazon and Meta (META) all announced significant nuclear contracts. Google partnered with Kairos Power LLC to deploy 500 MW of advanced nuclear projects beginning in 2030; Amazon said it was working with advanced nuclear developer X-energy and Dominion Energy Inc. (D) for advanced reactor projects; and Meta is seeking proposals for up to 4 GW of nuclear by the early 2030s.

"For Silicon Valley and its offshoots, nuclear is a sensible solution amid quickly steepening AI-related load forecasts," Tony Lenoir, an analyst with the S&P Global Commodity Insights Energy Research team, wrote in a January report.

Other energy sources

Beyond nuclear, natural gas will remain a key energy source in the portfolio of datacenter operators, according to a report from S&P Global Ratings. If 50% of incremental capacity comes from natural gas-fired units, including baseload and peak suppliers, the grid would require up to 50 GW of incremental generation supply. Up to 3 Bcf/d of this demand could be met with natural gas, Ratings said in the report.

Natural gas is attractive for the same reason nuclear is: Datacenters need uninterrupted, around-the-clock access to power. One key reason to keep natural gas in the rotation is that it can alleviate transmission bottlenecks, Exiger LLC CEO Brandon Daniels said.

"Even if renewable installations are available or there is the grid infrastructure and sustainable electricity, there are gaps in the grid that don't allow for sustainable transmission," Daniels said. "Those same gaps in the grid also can create intermittency issues with wind- and solar-powered installations."

Microsoft is working with Houston-based Enchanted Rock LLC, which provides electrical resiliency microgrids, to help supply its San Jose datacenter with power - including renewable natural gas - starting in 2026.

Renewable natural gas includes captured methane emitted by food, agricultural and animal waste, turning it into fuel while keeping it out of the atmosphere. While renewable natural gas only accounts for 1% of overall gas use, it could grow to more than 8% by 2050, according to an estimate from the Coalition for Renewable Natural Gas.

"Natural gas provides a very elegant, homegrown solution for balancing the grid and providing capacity during those peak hours where loads are high and we don't have enough renewable resources available to meet that need," said Allan Schurr, chief commercial officer of Enchanted Rock. "I think the pathway may be a little more meandering than what it was just a few years ago, but they're still focused on getting there."

'New levers to pull'

Beyond alternative energy sources, datacenter operators and tech companies will need to find "new levers to pull" for decarbonization, said West Monroe's Uyeno. In particular, Uyeno sees a window of opportunity for the most innovative tech giants to leverage their AI capabilities to optimize their supply chains and energy pipelines for sustainability.

"One new lever that's probably not tapped enough is, how do you use AI to evaluate datacenter operations and start to make it more efficient," Uyeno said. "Or, how does a datacenter look at all of its generation and load pool with AI to operate more efficiently?"

AI can be deployed to advance efforts to reach net-zero targets, streamline energy permitting and potentially abate emissions, according to Accenture projections. In one scenario, AI has a continued and lasting negative impact on net-zero trajectories. In another, it has a rapid positive impact, and in a third forecast, it increases emissions but has a positive impact on net-zero objectives in the medium-term.

Meta, for instance, used AI to run simulations to adjust how fans in its datacenters are used for cooling. Based on the simulations, Meta was able to reduce the fans' energy consumption by 20%.

"Another example is applying AI to novel formulations of concrete to reduce the carbon footprint in use in our datacenters," Blair Swedeen, global head of net-zero and sustainability at Meta, told audiences at Climate Week NYC. "That approach has reduced the carbon footprint of that concrete by about 40% ... and then we open source them to make them available to the broader community."

Experts emphasize that companies do not have to choose between sustainability and AI, or sustainability and profits. Both are a false dilemma.

"By taking manageable risks, you can reduce your capex by up to 40%, for instance, while producing the same amount of energy, and then you can use the savings to build carbon capture or whatever you need, or make your product more affordable for end customers," Maksim Sonin, an energy executive and projects fellow at Stanford University, told Market Intelligence. "AI, despite all this growing energy demand, is set to help us to drive things in this way."

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: 5 Overlooked 12% Yields To Supercharge Your Early Retirement
data.description:
To retire early, investors can take one of two paths. Either they can amass a large nest egg and gradually deplete it over time by selling down assets, such as shares in a diversified S&P 500 ETF, and use the proceeds to fund their living expenses, or they can live off of cash flow from income-generating assets that they own.

The second approach, if set up successfully, allows investors to never sell down any of their shares or assets and instead simply live off the cash flow that those assets produce. Plan A can also last indefinitely, provided one of two conditions are met. Either an investor has a large enough nest egg that even gradual depletion will allow it to comfortably last beyond their expected lifespan, or the growth in the underlying asset value offsets the depletion as they sell shares, thereby creating a self-sustaining fund that can support their living expenses indefinitely. Meanwhile, Plan B works as long as the passive income fully covers living expenses and that income is sustainable.

Another important consideration is the corrosive impact of inflation, which drives up living expenses over time. Therefore, the income source must also increase at least at the pace of inflation to remain sustainable.

While both approaches can work, I prefer the latter approach to the former, because dividends are much more consistent and dependable than stock market or asset appreciation performance, and therefore help to reduce the sequence of returns risk.

An easy way to implement this approach is simply to invest in the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD), which is broadly diversified into over 100 blue-chip dividend growth stocks, charges a very low 0.06% expense ratio, and has grown its payout consistently year after year at a pace that has easily exceeded inflation. One drawback to this approach, however, is that the fund only pays out a 3.6% dividend yield, meaning that investors still need to amass a large nest egg to be able to fully fund their living expenses through cash flow alone.

That said, this type of fund can be enhanced by diversifying into higher-yielding securities that can boost the portfolio’s yield while still allowing the strong dividend growth of SCHD to help keep the passive income stream growing faster than inflation. With that in mind, in this article, I am going to share five often overlooked investments that yield around 12% each and can help accelerate your early retirement timeline by providing access to what should be fairly sustainable payouts.

5 Overlooked 12% Yields

The first two investments are also broadly diversified ETFs, similar to SCHD, except they take a different approach. The first one is the NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI), and the second one is the NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF (QQQI). Both of these ETFs employ semi-actively managed call strategies, where they sell covered calls while also buying significantly out-of-the-money calls to help preserve some upside potential during bull markets while still generating very attractive yields. In fact, they target a 1% monthly yield, leading to about a 12% annualized distribution yield.

SPYI holds the underlying S&P 500 index, whereas QQQI holds the underlying Nasdaq 100 index. Additionally, they sell 1256 contracts, which qualify for tax-advantaged treatment. 60% of their gains are taxed at long-term capital gains rates, while only 40% are taxed at short-term gains rates, making them fairly tax-efficient high-yield funds. I think these are helpful diversification tools because SCHD and many other dividend growth ETFs with higher yields tend to have very low exposure to mega-cap tech stocks. In contrast, QQQI has roughly 50% exposure to mega-cap tech, and SPYI has about 33% exposure. This helps to create a more diversified portfolio while also enhancing income, offsetting the lower yields that many dividend growth ETFs, such as SCHD, tend to offer.

Meanwhile, another 12%-yielding investments that can further diversify the portfolio include the UBS ETRACS Gold Shares Covered Call ETN (GLDI), which provides gold (GLD) exposure while also generating high income. Like QQQI and SPYI, GLDI employs a monthly covered call strategy, which has generated a 12.06% yield over the past 12 months, while its share price has also appreciated by nearly 10%. Of course, this performance reflects a bull market for gold, so during down markets, GLDI will also decline. However, by incorporating it into a balanced portfolio and regularly rebalancing, GLDI can provide valuable diversification benefits.

Since gold tends to be relatively uncorrelated with the broader stock market, GLDI can serve as a ballast for the portfolio during tough times while continuing to generate attractive income, helping to accelerate early retirement plans. GLDI is particularly useful as a hedge against major geopolitical or macroeconomic events, which can drive investors into gold while stocks crash. In these scenarios, GLDI can stabilize the portfolio while also delivering monthly income.

A fourth 12%-yielding option for diversifying and strengthening an early retiree's portfolio is the NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund (NXG). It currently pays out a 12.26% distribution rate, with monthly payouts, similar to GLDI, SPYI, and QQQI.

At the moment, NXG trades at a 2.28% premium to NAV, which is fairly reasonable compared to previous periods where it traded at a much higher premium, including its 52-week high premium of 8.38%. However, it is slightly expensive since its 52-week average premium is just 0.6%. The fund employs a fairly prudent 21.84% leverage ratio, which should remain sustainable in all but the most extreme market crashes. It does have a 2.71% reported expense ratio, according to CEF Connect, but it holds some of the biggest blue-chip midstream companies, including Energy Transfer (ET), Williams Companies (WMB), ONEOK (OKE), MPLX (MPLX), Kinder Morgan (KMI), and Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) among its top holdings. Given its track record of paying out stable distributions each month, NXG should be a reliable source of attractive passive income, helping to drive strong income for an early retiree’s portfolio while offering exposure to high-quality midstream infrastructure assets.

Last but not least, complementing this list of funds is an individual stock, Morgan Stanley Direct Lending Fund (MSDL). It is a business development company that has one of the lower fee rates among externally managed BDCs. It also has an investment-grade balance sheet, a leverage ratio below 1x, and a portfolio almost entirely made up of first-lien senior secured loans, making it defensively positioned. Management is well-aligned, with Morgan Stanley (MS)—its parent company—owning about 10% of the equity. It pays out a 10% base dividend yield, but with the supplemental dividends it has been consistently paying, its annualized yield moves to 12%.

Given that MSDL trades roughly in line with net asset value, despite peers like Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL) and Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC) trading at much larger premiums to NAV while offering lower yields, I think MSDL is not only a compelling value but also an attractive risk-adjusted yield. This makes it a good fit for early retirees who want to preserve and even grow principal over time while also generating a very attractive income yield. Therefore, I think MSDL can be a great component of a diversified portfolio, like the one mentioned in this article.

Investor Takeaway

This article shares five overlooked 12% yields that can provide valuable diversification to an early retiree’s portfolio:

  1. SPYI offers a broad selection of blue-chip companies while generating a 12% annualized yield via monthly distributions.
  2. QQQI provides concentrated exposure to mega-cap tech stocks with a similar 12% monthly distribution.
  3. NXG offers diversified exposure to leading blue-chip midstream companies, along with a 12% annualized yield via monthly distributions.
  4. GLDI provides exposure to the diversification benefits of gold, while also paying out an attractive monthly distribution that annualizes to about 12%.
  5. MSDL gives the portfolio exposure to a diversified basket of first-lien senior secured floating-rate loans, with strong underwriting, an attractive valuation, and about a 12% annualized yield.

This portfolio is not necessarily designed to crush the market over time, but it is well-positioned to deliver a very lucrative cash flow stream for investors looking to retire on dividends. When coupled with a dividend growth ETF such as SCHD, it can create a well-diversified portfolio that pays out an attractive combination of current yield while also allowing payouts to grow over time to help keep pace with inflation. While we steer clear of funds like these in favor of building a portfolio of individual stocks to pursue greater alpha, we still employ these core principles of combining high current yield with strong dividend growth at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Buried Treasures: 4 Dramatically Undervalued REITs
data.description:
In October 2023, at about this time, REIT prices were bottoming. In an article that November, I wrote this:

After the brutal sell-off of the last two years, REITs are poised for a surge. The landscape is littered with quality REITs whose yields are at or near their all-time highs. Inflation appears to be under control, and nearly down to the Fed target of 2%. It is likely that interest rates will hold steady or drop in the foreseeable future.

Although inflation has crept back to 3.00%, the vast majority of investors are expecting one more rate cut this year, dropping the prime rate to 4.25%.

Both the falling rates and the resumption of low inflation make REIT yields more attractive. With "no-risk" 10-year treasury yields at about 4.4% and likely to fall, retirees with just a modest taste for risk can now find numerous REITs with highly competitive yields.

For the more risk-averse investors, value investors, and investors who depend on dividend income (such as retirees), this article identifies all the equity REITs that are at least 20% undervalued and yielding 5.15% or better. I will then narrow the list down according to dividend safety, balance sheet quality, and projected growth, to arrive at a group that should not only pay well but hold or handsomely increase their share value over the next year or two.

This approach was very successful last year, producing a 10-REIT portfolio that generated an annualized total return of 32.6%, outperforming the 17.8% posted by the Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ).

The Initial List

Here is a list of all the REITs currently yielding at least 5.15% or better (at least 75 basis points above the "no-risk" rate), while selling at least 20% below their Target Fair Value as determined by Hoya Capital. (NOTE: Yields and Discounts shift minute-by-minute with changes in the share price. By the time you read this, the companies on this list may have somewhat different yields.)

Company Ticker Sector Discount Yield
BRT Apartments (BRT) Apartment 22.5% 5.61%
Apple Hospitality (APLE) Hotel 35.8% 6.64%
Park Hotels (PK) Hotel 25.1% 8.09%
RLJ Lodging (RLJ) Hotels 26.6% 6.54%
Alexandria Real Estate (ARE) Medical 23.8% 5.31%
Healthpeak Properties (DOC) Medical 26.3% 6.15%
Global Medical (GMRE) Medical 22.5% 9.86%
Sabra Health Care (SBRA) Medical 28.6% 7.31%
CTO Realty Growth (CTO) Shopping Center 30.2% 8.07%
Realty Income (O) Net Lease 31.8% 5.72%
NNN REIT (NNN) Net Lease 19.2% 5.42%
Broadstone Net Lease (BNL) Net Lease 32.4% 7.15%
NETSTREIT (NTST) Net Lease 27.7% 5.66%
BXP (BXP) Office 26.0% 5.61%
Kilroy Realty (KRC) Office 20.8% 6.34%
City Office (CIO) Office 23.1% 8.00%
Highwoods Properties (HIW) Office 29.5% 6.92%
American Assets Trust (AAT) Office 24.7% 6.22%
Armada Hoffler (AHH) Office 27.7% 9.07%
Piedmont Office (PDM) Office 19.9% 6.93%
Plymouth Industrial (PLYM) Industrial 30.0% 5.84%
Crown Castle International (CCI) Cell Tower 27.3% 6.70%
Gaming and Leisure Properties (GLPI) Casino 24.4% 6.14%
Outfront Media (OUT) Billboard 28.3% 6.72%
Innovative Industrial Properties (IIPR) Cannabis 27.7% 10.30%
New Lake Capital Partners (OTCQX:NLCP) Cannabis 22.1% 10.78%
AFC Gamma (AFCG) Cannabis 23.9% 15.77%

Source: iREIT+Hoya Capital

Also Consider...

There are other REITs that narrowly missed on one criterion, but qualified with room to spare on the other.

  • VICI Properties (VICI)
  • Omega Healthcare (OHI)

However, OHI has a Dividend Safety grade of D, eliminating it from inclusion in this group.

No Sucker Yields, Thanks

Often, when a company is sporting a high dividend, it is precisely because the business is failing. The stock price is falling, and the dividend has not yet been cut, so the Yield looks really juicy. The investor who takes the bait of that high yield often regrets it, as the company slashes its dividend and COWhand investors flee, leaving the bait-taker with the worst of both worlds: reduced yield and dramatic share price loss.

Within the scope of this article, it's not feasible to examine every company, testing for sucker yields, but we can do some simple screens. First, we can screen for Dividend Safety, eliminating any company with a grade of D or worse, as assigned by Seeking Alpha Premium.

That eliminates the following candidates:

  • BRT (Safety grade D-)
  • GMRE (Safety grade F)
  • BXP (Safety grade D)
  • HIW (Safety grade D)
  • AHH (Safety grade D-)
  • CCI (Safety grade F)

and puts the following on the bubble, with a Dividend Safety grade of D+:

  • SBRA
  • AAT
  • GLPI
  • AFCG

No Sinking Ships, please

To me, there is nothing riskier than a company whose revenues are falling. These are the kinds that end up cutting their dividends.

We want stable, healthy companies, so next, we can eliminate any company whose FFO/share is expected to remain flat or fall, this year or next, according to consensus estimates, based on forecasts by the companies themselves, and the Wall Street analysts who follow them.

Ticker FFO '24 FFO '25* Growth FFO '26* Growth

PK 2.03 2.10 3.4% 2.10 0.0%
RLJ 1.53 1.59 3.9% 1.68 5.7%
ARE 9.29 9.57 3.0% 9.91 3.6%
DOC 1.86 1.94 4.3% 2.00 3.1%
CTO 1.89 2.00 5.8% NA NA
O 4.39 4.53 3.2% 4.62 2.0%
NNN 3.37 3.50 3.9% 3.56 1.7%
BNL 1.53 1.57 2.6% 1.60 1.9%
NTST 1.27 1.30 2.4% 1.28 (-1.5)%
KRC 4.09 4.09 0.0% 4.31 5.4%
CIO 1.09 1.13 3.7% 1.20 6.2%
PDM 1.43 1.43 0.0% 1.46 2.1%
PLYM 1.70 1.97 15.9% 2.12 7.6%
OUT 1.92 2.03 5.7% 2.16 6.4%

NLCP 1.99 1.99 0.0% NA NA
VICI 2.71 2.39 (-11.8)% 2.90 21.3%

AAT 2.08 2.22 6.7% 2.41 8.6%
GLPI 3.96 4.11 3.8% 4.39 6.8%
AFCG 1.30 1.30 0.0% 1.30 0.0%

Source: iREIT+Hoya Capital

* Estimated

This screen thus eliminates the following 10 REITs for flat or sliding revenues:

  • APLE
  • PK
  • CTO
  • NTST
  • KRC
  • PDM
  • IIPR
  • NLCP
  • VICI
  • AFCG

A Question of Balance

Another major risk factor for any company is its balance sheet. Companies that have high indebtedness, and/or hold their debts at high interest rates, struggle to grow and are more vulnerable to unexpected changes in business conditions.

Our list is down to 12 companies now, spread over 7 sectors. Of our prime candidates, only 9 remain, along with 3 on the bubble. Let's take a look at their key balance sheet metrics. We want to see most of these metrics equal to or better than the average REIT in their respective sectors. Red ink in two squares will eliminate the company from consideration.

Ticker Sector Debt Ratio Debt/EBITDA % Variable Rate
RLJ Hotel 59% 5.9 30.2
Average Hotel 39% 5.6 22.7
ARE Medical 38% 5.4 0.4
DOC Medical 38% 5.4 0.0
SBRA Medical 37% 5.8 9.9
Average Medical 28% 5.3 1.3
O Net Lease 40% 5.6 0.3
NNN Net Lease 36% 5.6 0.0
BNL Net Lease 38% 6.0 0.0
Average Net Lease 38% 5.7 3.5
CIO Office 68% 8.2 26.2
AAT Office 64% 6.3 61.3
Average Office 52% 7.5 7.2

Average Industrial 27% 5.6 3.7

GLPI Casino 38% 5.3 0.0
Average Casino 36% 4.7 3.1

Source: iREIT+Hoya Capital

This screen eliminates the following companies for shaky balance sheets:

  • RLJ
  • SBRA
  • CIO
  • AAT
  • PLYM
  • OUT
  • GLPI

Investor's bottom line

We are left with just 4 buried treasures. All carry a safe yield of over 5.15%, with stable revenues and sturdy balance sheets, and are at least 20% undervalued. They are listed below. "Implied Return" is the discount plus the yield.

Ticker Sector Discount % Yield % Implied Return
ARE Medical 23.8% 5.31% 29.11%
DOC Medical 26.3% 6.15% 32.45%
O Net Lease 31.8% 5.72% 37.52%
NNN Net Lease 19.2% 5.42% 24.62%

Source: iREIT+Hoya Capital

Just because a company appears on this list, that does not mean I recommend buying shares. You will need to do your own due diligence, as always, but hopefully, this list will save you a lot of time, by narrowing the field.

And remember, the opinion that matters most is yours. Because it's your money.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Elastic Q3 Earnings: Why Investors Are Buying Into The AI Growth Story
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Elastic (NYSE:ESTC) delivered a strong fiscal Q3 2025 earnings report that saw the stock jump 15% after hours.

What's particularly interesting to note is that the market right now is just looking for a reason to take profits off the table, as the risk-off mood is very prevalent.

With that in mind, it shows that investors are still seeking well-positioned growth stories and are happy to back companies that can show how they are benefitting from AI, and improving their underlying profitability.

Accordingly, Elastic, with a 5% net cash to market cap position, is an attractive investment at 41x forward free cash flow.

Rapid Recap

Back in November, I made a bullish call on ESTC where I stated,

I now believe, that in this brave new market, paying 46x next year's free cash flow for ESTC is a good entry point.

And it's interesting to note that since that day, roughly 90 days ago, the stock hasn't participated in much of the upside or the downside of the market.

Elastic's Near-Term Prospects

Elastic helps businesses search. That's the core of its offering.

Customers use Elastic to analyze their own data to deliver insights. This can be data logs or documents, rigid or fluid data.

Elastic's main product, Elasticsearch, makes it easy for companies to find insights.

On top of that, Elastic is also a big player in observability, which helps businesses monitor and troubleshoot their systems, even though that space is becoming very crowded.

Also, Elastic competes in cybersecurity which is even more crowded!

But essentially, the key to its offering is that they consolidate many different IT requirements onto one platform, platformization, which saves customers money while improving efficiency.

Indeed, from their earnings call, we heard the same refrain that they've been making for a while, that AI is a big growth driver and that customers continue to look for ways to consolidate their tech stack.

Given this background, let's now discuss its fundamentals.

Revenue Growth Rates of 16% Y/Y in Fiscal 2026

Not only did Elastic raise its full-year fiscal 2026 outlook yet again, but it also beat analysts' fiscal Q3 2025 revenues by more than 3 percentage points.

This is terrific and gives investors a lot of confidence that the business still has some fuel left in the tank and sets up its prospects for fiscal year 2026 to grow by at least 16% y/y, if not a little faster.

On top of that, keep in mind that fiscal Q4 2025 is not only up against the most challenging comparable of the prior year, but also, there are fewer days in fiscal Q4 2025 than in any of the first three quarters of fiscal 2025.

All in all, I'm reasonably confident that Elastic can grow its topline by around 16% y/y, which makes Elastic a rather interesting growth opportunity.

With that in mind, let's now discuss its valuation.

ESTC Stock Valuation -- 41x Forward Free Cash Flow

Elastic holds a net cash position of approximately 5% of its market cap, if we include the after-hours pop in its share price. This means that Elastic has a strong balance sheet that is supportive of the bull case.

Now, before we go further ahead, consider this quote from the earnings call,

[...] we expect to expand non-GAAP operating margin only modestly in fiscal '26 relative to our current non-GAAP operating margin guidance for fiscal '25. We expect these investments to be more front-end loaded in fiscal '26 as our sales kick off and engineering Global All Hands will both be in Q1 of FY '26. We will provide more detail on our Q4 earnings call.

Elastic's non-GAAP operating margins may reach 16% at some point in the next twelve months.

Even if not exactly in fiscal year 2026, it will be on a path toward this profit margin at some point in the next twelve months, even as we consider the fact that Elastic will be front-loading investment in its operations.

Therefore, we can see a path for Elastic's non-GAAP operating profits to reach about $280 million.

Now, keep in mind that Elastic likes to guide towards its adjusted free cash flow figure. However, this metric removes interest payments, as if interest payments is a non-existent pesky detraction.

However, from a clean free cash flow figure estimate, I believe that Elastic's free cash flow margin could reach around 17% in fiscal 2026.

Altogether, I believe that Elastic is able to get to about $300 million of clean free each flow at some point in the coming twelve months.

This leaves the stock priced at 41x forward free cash flow. A figure that is acceptable, for investors that want exposure to Elastic's growth engine.

Risk Factors to Consider

Back in summer 2024, Elastic had some sales execution issues, which caused investors to lose confidence in management's strategy. This has left this business in a penalty box and is now to some extent, a ''show-me'' story before investors get too excited about Elastic.

In other words, I believe it may take more than just 2 good quarters before investors fully buy back into Elastic's narrative.

On top of that, Elastic competes against the likes of Datadog (DDOG) and Splunk (which has now been acquired by Cisco (CSCO)) in the observability space and for analyzing logs.

Even though Elastic has a strong market share due to its open-source roots, none of its peers are pushovers either.

The Bottom Line

Elastic is proving that it belongs in the conversation when it comes to high-growth, AI-driven software companies. With a solid 16%+ revenue growth outlook, improving margins, and a strong balance sheet, this stock has the fundamentals to back up its momentum.

Sure, it faces tough competition, but its ability to consolidate IT needs into one platform gives it a real advantage.

At 41x forward free cash flow, it's not a steal, but for those looking to bet on continued growth and efficiency in enterprise search, observability, and security, Elastic looks like a compelling investment.

Investment return and principal value are historical and may fluctuate so that redemption value may be worth more or less than the original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than what is quoted. Call 1-866-AVE-MARIA or visit Ave Maria Mutual Funds for the most current month-end performance.

The Fund's exposure to dividend-paying common stocks and high-quality, short-duration bonds contributed to its outperformance versus the benchmark during the year. Notable performers included Texas Pacific Land Corporation (TPL), Truist Financial Corporation (TFC) and Coca-Cola Europacific Partners (CCEP), which increased by 115%, 24%, and 18%, respectively. Performance detractors include the common stocks of United Parcel Service, Inc. (UPS) and Genuine Parts Company (GPC), which declined by -15% and -13%, respectively. The Fund's longer-duration bond holdings saw low single-digit declines as interest rates increased.

In a twist of fate, interest rates on longer-dated bonds have increased as the Federal Reserve reduced short-term rates by 1% during the latter half of the year. A stronger-than-expected economy, significant fiscal deficits by the Federal Government and lingering inflation present challenges to further lower short-term interest rates this year. Corporate bond spreads have continued to narrow near historic lows. Investors should exercise caution when adding credit exposure at this time. This is particularly important for low credit quality bonds, where investors are not being adequately compensated for the additional credit risk. The Bond Fund will continue to be managed in a conservative manner by keeping bond maturities in the short-to-intermediate range and the credit quality high. Additionally, high-quality, dividend-paying common stocks continue to offer an attractive combination of income and price appreciation potential.

IMPORTANT INFORMATION FOR INVESTORS

(no longer held) and Genuine Parts Company (1.3%). Fund holdings are subject to change and should not be considered purchase recommendations. (1.7%), Illinois Tool Works, Inc. 2.65% due 11/15/26 (1.5%), U.S. Treasury Note 4.625% due 09/30/30 (1.5%), U.S. Treasury Note 4.50% due 11/15/25 (1.5%), U.S. Treasury Note 1.375% due 01/31/25 (1.5%), U.S. Treasury Note 4.125% due 09/30/27 (1.5%), U.S. Treasury Note 4.50% due 11/15/33 (1.5%), U.S. Treasury Note 4.25% due 06/30/29 (1.5%) and U.S. Treasury Note 2.875% due 06/15/25 (1.5%). The most current available data regarding portfolio holdings can be found on our website, www.avemariafunds.com. Current and future portfolio holdings are subject to risk.

The Adviser invests only in securities that meet the Fund's investment and religious requirements. The returns may be lower or higher than if decisions were based solely on investment considerations. The method of security selection may or may not be successful and the Fund may underperform or outperform the stock market as a whole. All mutual funds are subject to market risk, including possible loss of principal. The Fund's investments in small- and mid- capitalization companies could experience greater volatility than investments in large-capitalization companies. The Fund invests primarily in fixed income securities and as a result the Fund is also subject to the following risks: interest rate risk, credit risk, credit rating risk and liquidity risk. The investment performance assumes reinvestment of dividends and capital gains distributions. Performance data reflects certain fee waivers and reimbursements. Without such waivers, performance would have been lower. The Bloomberg Intermediate U.S. Govt./Credit Index is the benchmark index used for comparative purposes for this fund. The Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index is a broad-based benchmark that measures the investment grade, US dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market. The index includes Treasuries, government-related and corporate securities, fixed-rate agency MBS, ABS and CMBS (agency and non-agency). Performance data quoted represents past performance, which is no guarantee of future results. Investment return and principal value are historical and may fluctuate so that redemption value may be worth more or less than the original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than what is quoted. Performance data reflects certain fee waivers and reimbursements. Without such waivers, performance would have been lower. Call 1-866-AVE-MARIA or visit www.avemariafunds.com for the most current month-end performance.

For the year ended December 31, 2024, performance was driven primarily by significant contributions from the Real Estate, Financial and Industrial sectors. The Real Estate sector soared an impressive 180% for the year, driven entirely by its sole holding, Landbridge Company LLC (LB). The Financial sector rose 13%, driven by strong performance from its three holdings. Meanwhile, the Industrials sector gained 10%, largely fueled by the outstanding performance of GFL Environmental, Inc. (GFL), which climbed nearly 30% for the year.

The largest relative detractors of performance for the year were Materials, Information Technology and Health Care. The Materials sector was down -15%, with two of the three holdings posting negative returns. The Information Technology sector was down -68% due to the only holding in the sector, Cirata PLC (OTCPK:WANSF). In contrast, the Health Care sector edged up 4%, supported by its lone holding, Chemed Corporation (CHE).

The Fund will continue to be managed with a focus on companies that can grow their per-share economic earnings over a long period of time. The Fund privileges companies with durable and forecastable earnings, companies with an economic moat, and companies that generate high returns on invested capital.

IMPORTANT INFORMATION FOR INVESTORS

As of 12-31-24, the holding percentages of the stocks mentioned in this commentary are as follows: Landbridge Company LLC. (6.1), GFL Environmental, Inc., (8.0%), Cirata PLC (0.3%) and Chemed Corporation (1.1%). Fund holdings are subject to change and should not be considered purchase recommendations. There is no assurance that the securities mentioned remain in the Fund's portfolio or that securities sold have not been repurchased. The Fund's top ten holdings as of 12-31-24: eDreams ODIGEO SA (20.6%), APi Group Corporation (18.0%), Brookfield Corporation* (17.0%), DigitalBridge Group, Inc. (8.7%), GFL Environmental, Inc. (8.0%), Apollo Global Management (6.4%), SECURE Waste Infrastructure Corp. (6.2%), LandBridge Company LLC (6.1%), SigmaRoc PLC (4.2%) and Hermes International (1.2%). The most current available data regarding portfolio holdings can be found on our website, www.avemariafunds.com. Current and future portfolio holdings are subject to risk.

*Combination of Brookfield Corporation and Brookfield Wealth Solutions, Ltd.

The Adviser invests only in securities that meet the Fund's investment and religious requirements. The returns may be lower or higher than if decisions were based solely on investment considerations. The method of security selection may or may not be successful and the Fund may underperform or outperform the stock market as a whole. All mutual funds are subject to market risk, including possible loss of principal. The Fund's investments in small- and mid-capitalization companies could experience greater volatility than investments in large-capitalization companies. AVEAX is classified as non-diversified and may therefore invest a greater percentage of its assets in the securities of a limited number of issuers than a fund that is diversified. At times, the Fund may overweight a position in a particular issuer or emphasize investment in a limited number of issuers, industries or sectors, which may cause its share price to be more susceptible to any economic, business, political or regulatory occurrence affecting an issuer than a fund that is more widely diversified. The issuers that the Fund may emphasize will vary from time to time.

1 11/9/73 is the inception date for the oldest class of shares, Class A shares. Class I shares were first offered on 9/4/01; returns prior to this date are those of Class A shares and have not been adjusted for expenses; otherwise, returns would vary.

2 It is not possible to invest in an index. Index definitions can be found within the "What you should know before Investing" section on the last page of this fact sheet.

3“Net (what you pay)” represents the effect of a contractual fee waiver and/or expense reimbursement and is subject to change.

4 Listed holdings reflect the largest portions of the fund’s total and may change at any time. They are not recommendations to buy or sell any security. Data is expressed as a percentage of net assets and excludes cash and cash equivalents.

The performance data shown represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Returns for periods shorter than one year are cumulative, and results for other share classes will vary. Shares will fluctuate in value and, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance cited. Performance figures assume that all distributions are reinvested. For the most recent month-end performance, visit j hinvestments.com.

Market review and outlook

Most segments of the U.S. bond market sold off in the quarter, dampening total returns for the calendar year. Economic growth expectations improved, due in part to the outcome of the November U.S. elections. The U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) made two quarter-point interest-rate cuts, adding to the half-point reduction it enacted in September. Bonds nevertheless came under pressure from concerns about stubborn inflation, as well as Fed Chair Jerome Powell’s hawkish comments on the likely path of monetary policy in 2025.

U.S. Treasuries were adversely affected by these developments, with longer-term bonds experiencing the weakest results. The yield on the 10-year note (US10Y) surged from 3.81% at the end of September to 4.58% on December 31. Mortgage-backed securities, which tend to have elevated sensitivity to the direction of the Treasury market, sold off in response. Asset-backed securities (ABS) and commercial mortgage-backed securities outpaced MBS at a time in which investors displayed a preference for higher-yielding, credit-oriented categories.

Investment-grade corporates also suffered losses even though yield spreads continued to decline toward multi-year lows. High-yield bonds registered a narrow gain and outperformed investment-grade debt as the improving economic outlook provided firm support for investors’ appetite for risk. The category further benefited from its lower degree of interest-rate sensitivity.

Contributors and detractors

The fund’s yield curve positioning, primarily its overweight in the 5-to 10-year portion of the curve, hurt its fourth-quarter relative performance. The resulting shortfall was offset, to some extent, by a contribution from asset allocation. The fund benefited from its underweight in U.S. Treasuries and overweights in high-yield bonds, investment-grade corporates, and ABS. An overweight in agency MBS detracted. Security selection contributed to results, with the majority of the positive impact occurring in the investment-grade corporate space.

Portfolio changes

We maintained a stable portfolio positioning in the final three months of the year, with minor shifts that were driven largely by bottom-up selection. We added slightly to high yield and ABS, while making a modest reduction in U.S. Treasuries. We kept a significant overweight in agency MBS on the view that the category continued to offer compelling relative value and a source of higher quality. Duration (interest-rate sensitivity) remained neutral versus the benchmark, and the fund was overweight in intermediate-term bonds in anticipation of additional steepening in the yield curve.

In corporates, we maintained an overweight in financials on the belief that the sector was attractive on a relative basis. We also continued to focus on the non-cyclical and utilities sectors, while moving up in quality and focusing on shorter and intermediate maturities. The corporate yield curve was relatively flat, meaning that there was little benefit to taking on added risk by moving further out on the maturity spectrum. In addition, we believed an emphasis on higher-quality, shorter-dated debt could provide a measure of ballast if market conditions became less favorable. With corporate yield spreads at the tight end of the historic range, we maintained the view that income and security selection would be paramount. Although we didn’t look to increase the portfolio’s overall risk profile, we sought opportunities to add incremental yield through individual securities offering attractive relative value.
